assessments.title: Quiz: HTTP Requests and API Documentation
assessments.manifest_title: Quiz: HTTP Requests and API Documentation

questions.question_text: Which statement best describes the difference between the Internet and the World Wide Web?

choices.text: The Internet is the global network infrastructure, and the World Wide Web is a service that runs on top of it
The Internet is a service that runs on top of the World Wide Web
The Internet and the World Wide Web are the same thing
The World Wide Web only exists on local servers

questions.question_text: In a typical web application, which of the following acts as the client sending the request?
What is the main purpose of a GET request?

choices.text: To retrieve data from the server
To delete data from the server
To send data to the server
To update data in the server

questions.question_text: What is the main purpose of a POST request?

choices.text: To send data to the server
To retrieve data from the server
To delete data from the server

questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about GET requests is true?

choices.text: They are typically used to retrieve data
They are more secure than POST requests
They are intended to modify data on the server
They send data in the request body

questions.question_text: Which statement about HTTP requests is true?

choices.text: Every HTTP request should receive a response
Responses are optional if the request succeeds

questions.question_text: What does an HTTP response status code of 200 mean?

choices.text: The request was successful and the server returned the requested data
The server could not find the requested resource
The request was invalid due to client error
The server encountered an internal error

questions.question_text: What does an HTTP response status code of 404 mean?

choices.text: The server could not find the requested resource
The request was successful and the server returned the requested data
The resource was moved permanently to a different location

questions.question_text: Which part of a POST request usually contains the data being sent?
Why do developers use Postman?

choices.text: To test, simulate, and debug API endpoints by sending HTTP requests and checking responses
To write full applications instead of using a code editor
To replace version control tools like Git
To host and deploy backend servers

questions.question_text: When you type a URL into the address bar of your browser and press Enter, what type of HTTP request is sent to the server?
Which tab in your browser’s Developer Tools lets you see all the HTTP requests your browser is sending when loading a website?
Let's say you type the API URL into the search bar of your web browser, and press Enter. You see the screen shown below. What does this mean?

choices.text: The API was asleep, until you woke it up by sending a request. It'll wake up in about 30-60 seconds so hang tight!
You typed in the wrong API URL
You sent an invalid request to the API

questions.question_text:
Refer to the Countries App's Version 2 API Documentation. 

What is the base URL for all Version 2 API requests?
Which HTTP method should be used when submitting a form that adds a new user to the database?
According to the API Documentation, what type of HTTP request should you send to the /update-one-country-count API endpoint?
What should you send in the body of a request to the  /update-one-country-count API endpoint to update the count for "Japan"?
Which part of an API URL specifies what you want to access from the server?

choices.text: HTTP method (such as GET or POST)

questions.question_text: What is the data type of the response returned by the  /update-one-country-count  API endpoint?
What is the data type of the response you get from the /get-newest-user API endpoint?

You call the /get-newest-user API endpoint and get back the response below in a data variable:

[
  {
    "user_id": 42,
    "name": "John Smith",
    "country_name": "Canada",
    "email": "john@example.com",
    "bio": "Another bio"
  }
]

How do you access the name property from this response so you can render it to the page?

You send a GET request to the /get-all-saved-countries endpoint and get this array of objects in the response:

[
  { 
	  "country_name": "Japan"
  },
  { 
	  "country_name": "Germany" 
  },
  { 
	  "country_name": "Brazil" 
  } 
]

You want to display each saved country’s name, population, region, flag image, and capital on the page. What do you need to do next?

choices.text: Use each country_name from the /get-all-saved-countries response to find the full country details from the REST Countries API. That way you can get the population, region, flag, and capital for each saved country and display them on the page.
Use the data from /get-all-saved-countries—it already includes everything you need
Send a POST request to /save-one-country for each country
Try accessing properties like population and flag directly from the /get-all-saved-countries response

questions.question_text: What does JSON stand for, and what does it represent?

choices.text: JavaScript Object Notation — a lightweight format for storing and exchanging data, structured similarly to JavaScript objects and arrays
Java Syntax Object Notation — a programming language used to write backend code
Java Serialized Object Network — a database format for storing records
JavaScript Oriented Network — a protocol for sending HTTP requests

questions.question_text: Which request body is correctly formatted JSON for the /add-one-user endpoint?

assessments.title: Quiz: Creating Tables with SQL
assessments.module_title: Week 5: Intro to SQL: Creating & Querying Tables

questions.question_text: What does SQL stand for?
Which SQL clause is used to get specific columns from a table?
What does the wildcard * mean in a SELECT query?

choices.text: Select all columns from a table
Delete all rows from a table

questions.question_text: What is the purpose of the WHERE clause?

choices.text: To specify which rows should be returned or affected by a query
To define the data type of a column

questions.question_text: Which clause do you use to create a new table?
Which SQL clause would you use to add additional rows of data into a table?
Which SQL clause is used to modify existing rows of data in a table?
Which SQL clause is used to remove rows of data from a table?
Which SQL clause would you use to view all the data in a table named orders?
In SQL, what symbols do you type to write a single-line comment?
What is the purpose of the semicolon ; in SQL?

choices.text: To mark the end of a SQL statement
To separate columns in a table
To select all columns from a table

questions.question_text: Which of these is not a SQL data type?
If you want to prevent duplicate values in a column, which constraint would you use?
With SQL, how do you select a column named instagram_handle from a table named pets?
With SQL, how do you select all the records from a table named pets where the value of the column instagram_handle is '@snufflesnotmuffles'?
You want to select all columns for products that cost more than $1.50. Which query would you use?

choices.text: SELECT price FROM products WHERE price = 1.50;

questions.question_text: You accidentally typed the wrong email for a customer. Which query would you run to fix it?

choices.text: UPDATE customers SET email = 'new.email@example.com' WHERE customer_id = 2;
SELECT email FROM customers WHERE customer_id = 2;

questions.question_text: You want to remove all orders placed by the customer with customer_id = 3. Which SQL statement would you use?

choices.text: UPDATE orders SET customer_id = NULL WHERE customer_id = 3;

questions.question_text:
You run the following query: 

INSERT INTO products (product_name, price, inventory, description)
VALUE ('Choco Chip', 2.50, 10, 'Delicious cookies');
PostgreSQL returns this error message:
ERROR: syntax error at or near "VALUE"
Why did the query fail?

choices.text: Because the keyword should be VALUES instead of VALUE
Because INSERT INTO cannot be used with text values
Because the table products does not exist

questions.question_text:
You run the following queries: 

SELECT * FROM orders

SELECT name, email FROM customers;

PostgreSQL returns this error message:
ERROR: syntax error at or near "SELECT"
Why did the query fail?

choices.text: Because there is a missing semicolon after the first SELECT statement
Because SELECT cannot be used on multiple tables
Because the column names must be in quotes

questions.question_text:
You run the following query:

INSERT INTO customers (name, email)
VALUES (John, 'john@example.com');

PostgreSQL returns this error message:

ERROR: syntax error at or near "John"

Why did this error happen?

choices.text: The value John is missing single quotation marks, so PostgreSQL thinks it’s a column or keyword.
The column name cannot accept text values.
SQL does not allow inserting both name and email in the same query.
You forgot to add a semicolon at the end of the query.

assessments.title: Quiz: Querying Tables with SQL
assessments.module_title: Week 5: Intro to SQL: Creating & Querying Tables

questions.question_text: How do you select only the name and price columns from the groceries table?
Which operators allow you to check multiple conditions in a WHERE clause?
Which query selects all organic fruits from the groceries table?

choices.text: SELECT * FROM groceries WHERE category = 'Fruits' AND is_organic = true;
SELECT * FROM groceries WHERE category = 'Fruits' OR is_organic = true;
SELECT * FROM groceries WHERE is_organic = true;

questions.question_text: Which query selects groceries that are either condiments or beverages?

choices.text: SELECT * FROM groceries WHERE category = 'Condiments' OR category = 'Beverages';
SELECT * FROM groceries WHERE category = 'Condiments' AND category = 'Beverages';

questions.question_text: Which SQL operator lets you find rows where a text column contains a specific pattern?
How do you select all groceries where the name contains "Bread" anywhere in the name?

choices.text: SELECT * FROM groceries WHERE name LIKE '%Bread%';
SELECT * FROM groceries WHERE name CONTAINS 'Bread';

questions.question_text: Which clause allows you to eliminate duplicate results from a query?
How do you select all distinct values from the categories column from the groceries table?
Which SQL clause sorts query results?
How do you select groceries ordered by price from lowest to highest?

choices.text: SELECT * FROM groceries ORDER BY price ASC;
SELECT * FROM groceries ORDER BY price DESC;
SELECT * FROM groceries SORT BY price ASC;
SELECT * FROM groceries ORDER price LOWEST;

questions.question_text: How do you select all groceries ordered by quantity from highest to lowest?

choices.text: SELECT * FROM groceries ORDER BY quantity DESC;
SELECT * FROM groceries ORDER BY quantity ASC;
SELECT * FROM groceries SORT BY quantity DESC;
SELECT * FROM groceries ORDER quantity DESC;

questions.question_text: Which SQL clause limits the number of results returned?
How do you select the top 5 most expensive groceries?

choices.text: SELECT * FROM groceries ORDER BY price DESC LIMIT 5;
SELECT * FROM groceries ORDER BY price ASC LIMIT 5;
SELECT * FROM groceries TOP 5 ORDER BY price DESC;
SELECT * FROM groceries LIMIT 5 ORDER BY price DESC;

questions.question_text: You want to rename the column price to cost in your query result. Which SQL query does this correctly?

choices.text: SELECT name, price AS cost FROM groceries;
SELECT name, price TO cost FROM groceries;
SELECT name, price RENAME cost FROM groceries;
SELECT name, price LIKE cost FROM groceries;

questions.question_text:
You create a Users table with the following schema:

CREATE TABLE users (
    user_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    email VARCHAR UNIQUE NOT NULL,
    name VARCHAR NOT NULL
);

Then you run this command:

INSERT INTO users (email, name)
VALUES
    ('laura@anniecannons.com', 'Laura'),
    ('laura@anniecannons.com', 'Catie');

PostgreSQL returns this error message:

ERROR: duplicate key value violates unique constraint "users_email_key"

Why did this error happen?

choices.text: The email column was defined as UNIQUE, and you tried to insert the same email twice.
The email column was defined as NOT NULL.
The name column cannot have two different values for the same email.
The user_id column must always match the name column.

questions.question_text: Which column definition automatically gives each new row a uniquely identifying number in a table?

In the SQL error message below, what part tells you which column caused the problem?

ERROR: duplicate key value violates unique constraint "users_email_key"
Which of the following constraints would prevent two rows from having the same value?

PostgreSQL returns this error:

ERROR: null value in column "email" violates not-null constraint

What does this error tell you?

choices.text: A value was missing for the email column
The email already exists in the table

assessments.title: Quiz: Relational Databases
assessments.module_title: Week 5: Intro to SQL: Creating & Querying Tables

questions.question_text: What is a database?

choices.text: An organized collection of data stored electronically
A special type of server that only stores HTML files
A collection of files stored on your personal computer
A programming language used to build websites

questions.question_text: What’s the main difference between a server and a database?

choices.text: A database stores data, while a server handles requests for that data
A server stores the data, while the database delivers it
A server and a database are exactly the same thing
A database can only work on a personal computer, not with a server

questions.question_text: What is data governance concerned with?

choices.text: Ensuring data is secure, private, accurate, and responsibly managed
The hardware used to store a database
Writing server-side code to fetch data

questions.question_text: What is a relational database?

choices.text: A database that organizes data into tables with rows and columns
A database that stores data as unstructured documents
A database that only stores numbers and strings
A type of database that cannot be queried with SQL

questions.question_text: What is the main purpose of a primary key in a relational database table?

choices.text: To uniquely identify each row so there are no duplicates
To allow a column to have duplicate values
To store sensitive user data like passwords
To give users access to a server

questions.question_text: What is a foreign key used for?

choices.text: To link a row in one table to a primary key in another table
To identify rows in the same table
To automatically back up a database

questions.question_text: What does the NOT NULL constraint do in a table column?

choices.text: It requires every row to have a value in that column (no empty values)
It prevents duplicate values from being entered in that column
It automatically generates a unique number in order for the column
It links the column to a primary key in another table

questions.question_text: What does the UNIQUE constraint do in a table column?

choices.text: It ensures that every value in a column is different from the others
It requires every row to have a value in that column (no empty values)
It assigns a default value to a column when none is given
It automatically makes the column a primary key

questions.question_text:
Scenario:

You are designing a Users table. You have these requirements:

  1. Every user must have an email address.

  2. No two users can have the same email address.

Which constraints should you use for the email column?
In SQL, which of the following are data types you might use in a column of a table?

Scenario:

You are designing a Users table that needs to track whether a user is subscribed to the email newsletter. Which data type should you use for the is_subscribed column?

Scenario:

You are designing an Orders table. You need a column to store the quantity of items purchased in each order. Which data type should you use for the quantity_purchased column?
What is the main difference between VARCHAR and TEXT in a relational database?

choices.text: VARCHAR is used for shorter strings with a defined maximum length, while TEXT is used for longer, unlimited-length strings
TEXT is used for shorter strings with a defined maximum length, while VARCHAR is used for longer, unlimited-length strings
TEXT is used for numbers, while VARCHAR is used for strings
TEXT automatically enforces uniqueness, while VARCHAR does not

questions.question_text: When should you use VARCHAR instead of TEXT in a database?

choices.text: When you want to limit the length of the string and avoid storing endlessly long strings
When you want to store numbers instead of strings
When the string might be very long and you don’t know the maximum length
When you want to store a Boolean value

questions.question_text:
Scenario:

You are designing a Customers table that will have columns named first_name, last_name, and email. Which data type is best for these columns?
What does the SERIAL data type do in a relational database?

choices.text: Automatically generates a unique, incrementing number for each new row
Stores very large text values without a maximum length
Ensures a column cannot have empty (NULL) values
Links a column to a primary key in another table

questions.question_text:
Scenario: 

You are creating a Users table. You want the user_id column to automatically assign a new, unique number every time a new user is added. Which data type should you use?
Why are constraints like NOT NULL and UNIQUE important in a database?

choices.text: They help keep data accurate and consistent by preventing missing or duplicate values
They replace the need for a server
They automatically write SQL for you

questions.question_text: Which of the following follows proper naming conventions for a table or column name in a relational database?

assessments.title: Quiz: Upserting Tables with SQL
assessments.module_title: Week 5: Intro to SQL: Creating & Querying Tables

questions.question_text: What does the term “upsert” mean in SQL?

choices.text: Insert a new row if it doesn’t exist, or update the existing row if there’s a conflict
Insert multiple rows at the same time
Update all rows in a table
Replace a table with new data

questions.question_text: What causes a conflict during an INSERT when using ON CONFLICT?

choices.text: Running two SELECT queries at once

questions.question_text:
Let's say you create a cats table: 

CREATE TABLE cats (
  id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
  name VARCHAR NOT NULL UNIQUE,
  age INTEGER NOT NULL
);

Which column in the cats table can cause a conflict during an insert?

What does this query do if a cat named "Pixel" already exists?

INSERT INTO cats (name, age)
VALUES ('Pixel', 1)
ON CONFLICT (name) DO NOTHING;

choices.text: It skips the insert and leaves the table unchanged

questions.question_text: Which ON CONFLICT option updates an existing row instead of inserting a duplicate?

Let's say you run this SQL query: 

INSERT INTO cats (name, age) 
VALUES ('Pixel', 1)
ON CONFLICT (name) 
DO UPDATE SET age = cats.age + 1
RETURNING age;

 

What does this part of the query do?

SET age = cats.age + 1

choices.text: Increases the existing cat’s age by 1
Creates a new cat with age 1

questions.question_text:
Let's say you run this SQL query: 

INSERT INTO cats (name, age) 
VALUES ('Pixel', 1)
ON CONFLICT (name) 
DO UPDATE SET age = cats.age + 1
RETURNING age;

 

What does the RETURNING keyword do?

choices.text: Returns values from the inserted or updated row

questions.question_text: Why is RETURNING useful when using ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE?

choices.text: It lets you see the updated values immediately
It inserts multiple rows at once
It replaces the need for a SELECT statement forever

questions.question_text:
What will happen if you run this query when Egypt already exists in the country_counts table?

INSERT INTO country_counts (country_name, count)
VALUES ('Egypt', 1)
ON CONFLICT (country_name)
DO UPDATE SET count = country_counts.count + 1;

choices.text: A new row for Egypt will be added
The query will fail with an error
Egypt’s count will be reset to 1

questions.question_text: Why is the country_name column marked as UNIQUE in the country_counts table?

choices.text: So each country has only one count
So the table can store text values
So the table allows duplicate country names

questions.question_text: What happens when you insert a row with no conflict on a UNIQUE column?

choices.text: A new row is inserted normally

questions.question_text: Which real-world app behavior is best matched to using an upsert?

choices.text: Tracking how many times a country page is viewed

assessments.title: Quiz: SQL Joins
assessments.module_title: Week 6: SQL Continued: Aggregate Functions & Joins

questions.question_text: Which SQL clause explains how the two tables (owners and pets) are related in this SQL JOIN?
What SQL clause can you use to rename a column in the result of a query?
Which type of JOIN completes this query if we want to include only matches between pets and owners?
Which type of JOIN completes this query if we want to include all pets and owners, regardless of whether they have a match?
Which type of JOIN completes this query if we want to include all owners, even if they don’t have a pet?
Which type of JOIN completes this query if we want to include all pets, even if they don’t have an owner?

assessments.title: Quiz: Aggregate Functions in SQL
assessments.module_title: Week 6: SQL Continued: Aggregate Functions & Joins

questions.question_text:
What does this query return?

SELECT COUNT(*) FROM employees;

choices.text: The total number of employees in the table
The total salary of all employees

questions.question_text: Which query counts employees who earn more than 80,000?

choices.text: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM employees WHERE salary > 80000;
SELECT SUM(salary) FROM employees WHERE salary > 80000;
SELECT AVG(salary) FROM employees WHERE salary > 80000;

questions.question_text:
What does this query calculate?

SELECT SUM(salary) FROM employees;

choices.text: The total salary cost of all employees

questions.question_text: Which query finds the total salary cost of employees in the Engineering department?

choices.text: SELECT SUM(salary) FROM employees WHERE department = 'Engineering';
SELECT COUNT(salary) FROM employees WHERE department = 'Engineering';
SELECT AVG(salary) FROM employees WHERE department = 'Engineering';
SELECT SUM(department) FROM employees WHERE department = 'Engineering';

questions.question_text:
What does this query return?

SELECT AVG(salary) FROM employees;

choices.text: The average salary of all employees
The total salary of all employees

questions.question_text:
What is the purpose of ROUND() in this query?

SELECT ROUND(AVG(salary)) FROM employees WHERE department = 'Marketing';

choices.text: It rounds the average salary to the nearest whole number
It counts the number of Marketing employees
It removes decimal salaries from the table

questions.question_text:
What does this query return?

SELECT MIN(salary) FROM employees;

choices.text: The lowest salary in the company
The highest salary in the company

questions.question_text: Which query finds the highest salary in the company?

What does GROUP BY department do in this query?

SELECT department, COUNT(*) FROM employees GROUP BY department;

choices.text: It groups employees by department so the count is calculated per department
It sorts employees alphabetically by department

questions.question_text: Which query shows the highest salary in each department?

choices.text: SELECT department, MAX(salary) FROM employees GROUP BY department;

questions.question_text:
What does this query return?

SELECT department, SUM(salary) FROM employees GROUP BY department;

choices.text: The total salary cost per department
The total salary of the entire company

questions.question_text:
What does ORDER BY total_payroll DESC do in this query?

SELECT department, SUM(salary) AS total_payroll FROM employees GROUP BY department ORDER BY total_payroll DESC LIMIT 1;

choices.text: Sorts departments from highest total payroll to lowest
Filters out departments with low payroll

questions.question_text:
Why is LIMIT 1 necessary in this query?

SELECT department, SUM(salary) AS total_payroll FROM employees GROUP BY department ORDER BY total_payroll DESC LIMIT 1;

choices.text: Because we only want the department with the highest total payroll
Because SUM only works when limited to one row
Because GROUP BY only allows one result

assessments.title: Quiz: Node & Express
assessments.module_title: Week 9: Node and Express and SQL

questions.question_text: How do we run a file with Node?
What is process.argv in Node.js?

choices.text: An array containing arguments passed in when running the Node file on the command line
A built-in variable for server settings

questions.question_text: How do we read data from a file in Node.js?
How do we turn a JSON string into a JavaScript object?
How do we turn a JavaScript object into a JSON string?
What is package.json used for in a Node project?

choices.text: To store information and dependencies for the project

questions.question_text: What does "type": "module" do in package.json?

choices.text: Enables use of ES module syntax like the import keyword
Declares the app as a library

questions.question_text:
In a file path, what does ../ mean?

For example, what does the ../ mean in this file path? 

import "../components/CountryDetails.jsx"

In a file path, what does ./ mean?

For example, what does the ./ mean in this file path? 

import "./components/CountryDetails.jsx"
What is Express?

choices.text: A Node.js framework that makes it easier to build web servers and APIs
A cloud service for hosting servers online

questions.question_text: What is a web server?

choices.text: A computer that listens for and responds to HTTP requests

questions.question_text: What is an API?

choices.text: A collection of endpoints for interacting with data

questions.question_text: How do we handle a GET request in Express?
How do we write an API endpoint URL to get a specific user by their ID in Express?
How do we create a package.json file in a Node project?
What is a .gitignore file used for?

choices.text: To tell Git which files or folders not to track
To track changes in ignored files

questions.question_text:
Which statement is true about the following two code snippets? 

Code Snippet 1. const fs = require('fs')
Code Snippet 2. import fs from 'fs'

choices.text: Snippet 1 uses the older CommonJS syntax; Snippet 2 uses the modern ES Module syntax
Snippet 1 and 2 are both modern syntax
Snippet 1 uses the modern ES Module syntax; Snippet 2 uses the older CommonJS syntax
Both snippets are outdated and should not be used in Node.js

questions.question_text: What does res.send() do in an Express route handler?

choices.text: Sends a response back to the client that made the request
Sends a file to the browser

questions.question_text: What does req.params contain in Express?

choices.text: Dynamic parameters from the request URL
All the headers from the request
Data from the body of a POST request
The HTTP method of the request

questions.question_text:
When handling a GET request like this in Express:

app.get('/get-message', (req, res) => {
  res.send('Hello!');
});

 

What do req and res stand for?

choices.text: req is the request from the client, and res is the response the server sends back
req is the server, and res is the route name
req is the route, and res is the response text
req is the response the server sends back, and res is the request from the client

assessments.title: Quiz: Express & SQL
assessments.module_title: Week 10: Node, Express, SQL, and APIs

questions.question_text: What is a web server?

choices.text: A computer that listens for and responds to HTTP requests

questions.question_text: What is an API?

choices.text: A collection of endpoints for interacting with data

questions.question_text: What is Express?

choices.text: A Node.js framework that makes it easier to build web servers and APIs
A cloud service for hosting servers online

questions.question_text: What is package.json used for in a Node project?

choices.text: To store information about a project and its list of dependencies

questions.question_text: What does "type": "module" do in package.json?

choices.text: Enables use of ES module syntax like the import keyword
Declares the app as a library

questions.question_text: What is a .gitignore file used for?

choices.text: To tell Git which files or folders not to track
To track changes in ignored files

questions.question_text: What command do we use to run our backend server/API so that it can listen for HTTP requests and send back responses?
What command do we use to start and view our React frontend project in the browser?

In a file path, what does ../ mean?

For example, what does the ../ mean in this file path? 

import "../components/CountryDetails.jsx"

In a file path, what does ./ mean?

For example, what does the ./ mean in this file path? 

import "./components/CountryDetails.jsx"
How do we turn a JSON string into a JavaScript object?
How do we turn a JavaScript object into a JSON string?
Which HTTP method should you use to get data from the database?
Which HTTP method should you use to send data to the database?
How do we handle a GET request in Express?
How do we handle a POST request in Express?

When handling a request like this in Express:

app.get('/get-all-animals', (req, res) => {
  const animals = await getAllAnimals();
  res.json(animals)
});

 

What do req and res stand for?

choices.text: req is the request from the client, and res is the response the server sends back
req is the server, and res is the route name
req is the route, and res is the response text
req is the response the server sends back, and res is the request from the client

questions.question_text: How do we write an API endpoint URL to get a specific user by their ID in Express?
In Express, what does req.params contain?

choices.text: Dynamic parameters from the request URL
All the headers from the request
Data from the body of a POST request
The HTTP method of the request

questions.question_text: In Express, what does req.body contain?

choices.text: The data sent by the client in the request body
The dynamic URL parameters from the request
The headers from the HTTP request
The query string values from the URL

questions.question_text: Which SQL query would be used to insert a new animal into the database?

choices.text: INSERT INTO animals (name, category, can_fly, lives_in) VALUES ($1, $2, $3, $4)
ADD INTO animals (name, category, can_fly, lives_in) VALUES ($1, $2, $3, $4)
CREATE INTO animals (name, category, can_fly, lives_in) VALUES ($1, $2, $3, $4)
UPDATE animals (name, category, can_fly, lives_in) SET ($1, $2, $3, $4)

questions.question_text: In the query db.query("SELECT * FROM animals WHERE name = $1", [name]), what does $1 represent?

choices.text: A placeholder for the actual value passed into the query
The ID of the first animal

questions.question_text: What is the difference between res.send() and res.json()?

choices.text: res.send() sends a String, and res.json() sends a JavaScript object as JSON
res.send() is for errors, and res.json() is for success
res.send() sends data to the database, and res.json() sends it to the browser
There is no difference — they both send the same thing in the same way

questions.question_text:
What is the best way to send the response for this API endpoint? 

app.get("/get-all-animals", async (req, res) => {
  const animals = await getAllAnimals();
  // choose the best response method here
});

Hint: You are sending an array of animal objects in the response, not just a String message.

What is the best way to send the response for this API endpoint? 

app.post("/add-one-animal", async (req, res) => {
  const newAnimal = req.body;
  await addOneAnimal(newAnimal);
  // choose the best response method here
});

Hint: You're sending a success message to confirm that an animal was added to the database.
